answer:
The option is an asset-or-nothing put that pays the asset price if the index is down more than 40% at maturity, meaning the strike price is effectively 60% of the current index, or 600. Using the Black-Scholes formula for an asset-or-nothing put, the price per option is \( S_0 e^{-qT} N(-d_2) \), where \( d_2 = \frac{\ln(S_0/K) + (r - q - \sigma^2/2)T}{\sigma\sqrt{T}} \).  
Calculating gives \( d_2 \approx 0.221 \), so \( N(-d_2) \approx 0.4125 \), and the price per option is about \( 1000 e^{-0.02} \times 0.4125 \approx 404.3 \), so for one million units the total is about 4.04 million, closest to 4
